purpose: First H100x4 hybrid bring-up + 10 req smoke + diagnosis of CPU throughput parity vs dev
host: H100 80GB x4 + Xeon Platinum 8480+ (96 vCPU KVM, 1 NUMA, 944 GB RAM)

key_findings.boot: OK after rebuild (CUDA 13 graph capture issue resolved by recompile)
key_findings.abort_capacity_stuck: not reproduced — 10/10 success, slot cycle clean
key_findings.core_pin: C++ init_cpu_threads_env returned with 96 OMP workers 1:1 pinned to PSR 0..95
key_findings.cpu_throughput_per_req: ≈ 2.47 tok/s (mean TPOT 405 ms inverse). dev (i9-12900KF) was 2.3 tok/s for the same model. Practically equal.
key_findings.cpu_util_pattern: all 96 PSRs receive threads, ~70% util each. ~67 cpu-units total vs dev 16 cpu-units. 4.2x cpu budget but no throughput gain.
key_findings.root_cause_hypothesis: decode is memory-bandwidth bound: 7B BF16 weight (~14 GB) is read once per token, and KVM-guest DDR5 BW is similar to dev's DDR5. Adding cores past the BW limit gives no speedup. AMX not effective because compute is not the bottleneck. attention head=28 → IPEX paged-attn parallelism caps at 28 anyway.

implications.cpu_max_num_seqs_principle: cpu_max_num_seqs=1 is throughput-pessimal in BW-bound regime (no weight reuse). batching N seqs would reuse the single weight read across seqs.
implications.amx_value: AMX value materialises only when compute > BW (int8 / w8a8 quant, larger batch).
implications.model_scaling: larger models still BW-bound but absolute decode time grows linearly; ratio of CPU vs GPU may improve as the model becomes large enough that GPU is also slower per token.
next_actions: Run 500-prompt main with throughput-adaptive + cpu-first to characterise the adaptive routing behaviour with a slow CPU.
Try int8/w8a8 quantised 7B to test AMX kernel path and BW relief.
Try Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct to see whether the GPU/CPU ratio shifts in hybrid's favour at large scale.
